content.problemStatement:
You are given an array with NNN integers: A[1],A[2],…,A[N]A[1], A[2], \ldots, A[N]A[1],A[2],…,A[N] (where NNN is even). You are allowed to permute the elements however you want. Say, after permuting the elements, you end up with the array A′[1],A′[2],…,A′[N]A'[1], A'[2], \ldots, A'[N]A′[1],A′[2],…,A′[N]. Your goal is to maximize the following sum:

∣A′[1]−A′[2]∣ + ∣A′[3]−A′[4]∣ + ... + ∣A′[N−1]−A′[N]∣|A'[1] - A'[2]| \ + \ |A'[3] - A'[4]| \ + \ ... \ + \ |A'[N - 1] - A'[N]|∣A′[1]−A′[2]∣ + ∣A′[3]−A′[4]∣ + ... + ∣A′[N−1]−A′[N]∣Here, ∣x∣|x|∣x∣ denotes the absolute value of xxx.

You have to print the maximum sum achievable.


Input Format

The first line contains TTT, the number of test cases.
Each test case starts with an integer NNN in the first line.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space separated integers, denoting the values of array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output the maximum sum achievable in a new line.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases <= 2 * 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N in the first line. The second line of each test case contains N space-separated integers, denoting the values of array A.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the maximum sum achievable in a new line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: The original array is {1, -3, 2, -3}. Suppose you permute it and get the array {-3, 2, 1, -3}. Then the corresponding sum would be |-3 - 2| + |1 - (-3)| = 5 + 4 = 9.

Explanation: The original array is {-1, 5, 3, 4, 2, 6}. Suppose you permute it and get the array {-1, 6, 2, 5, 3, 4}. Then the corresponding sum would be |-1 - 6| + |2 - 5| + |3 - 4| = 7 + 3 + 1 = 11. But suppose you permute it differently and get the array {6, -1, 5, 2, 4, 3}. Then the corresponding sum would be |6 - (-1)| + |5 - 2| + |4 - 3| = 7 + 3 + 1 = 11. You can check that you cannot do any better, and hence the answer is 18.

Explanation: The original array is {10, 20, 30, 40}. Suppose you permute it and get the array {10, 40, 20, 30}. Then the corresponding sum would be |10 - 40| + |20 - 30| = 30 + 10 = 40. But suppose you permute it differently and get the array {40, 10, 30, 20}. Then the corresponding sum would be |40 - 10| + |30 - 20| = 30 + 10 = 40. You can check that you cannot do any better, and hence the answer is 60.

solution.approach: To maximize the sum of absolute differences, we need to pair the largest numbers with the smallest numbers. First, sort the array in ascending order. Then, pair the first element with the last element, the second element with the second last element, and so on. Calculate the absolute difference for each pair and sum them up.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N log N) — due to sorting the array for each test case
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — for sorting the array in place

code.Python3:
import sys

for _ in range(int(input())):
    N = int(input())
    A = list(map(int, input().split()))
    A.sort()
    sum = 0
    for i in range(N // 2):
        sum += abs(A[i] - A[N - 1 - i])
    print(sum)
